event_preview:
Sebring International Raceway is a 3.74-mile (6.02 km), 17-turn road course built on the former Hendricks Army Air Field in Sebring, Florida - one of the oldest and most gruelling permanent circuits in North America. Originally laid out on the wartime runways and perimeter roads of the airfield, the track retains its characteristically rough, bumpy surface that punishes both machinery and drivers over the course of a 12-hour endurance contest. Since its first running in 1952, the Twelve Hours of Sebring has established itself as one of the world's premier motorsports events, with winning drivers ranging from Juan Manuel Fangio and Mario Andretti to Tom Kristensen - the circuit's all-time winningest driver with six overall victories.

This year marks the 74th running of the event, with a field of 55 cars across four classes - 11 GTP, 12 LMP2, 13 GTD PRO and 19 GTD. A headline storyline entering the weekend is Porsche's bid for back-to-back GTP wins to open the 2026 season, having swept both GTP and GTD PRO at Sebring in 2025. A notable debut comes courtesy of Pfaff Motorsports, who will give the new Lamborghini Temerario GT3 its global race debut in GTD PRO, with an all-factory driver lineup of Andrea Caldarelli, Sandy Mitchell and Franck Perera.

The WeatherTech Championship track records by class stand as follows: GTP - 1:45.836 (Pipo Derani, Cadillac V-Series.R, 2023); LMP2 - 1:48.311 (Giedo van der Garde, ORECA LMP2 07, 2022); GTD PRO - 1:58.714 (Jack Hawksworth, Lexus RC F GT3, 2024); GTD - 1:58.710 (Daniel Serra, Ferrari 488 GT3, 2018).
